text: Suppose we have the following property files and code. What values are printed on lines 8 and 9?
explanation: For locale "fr" there is no Penguin_fr.properties, so the default locale "en_US" is used → Penguin_en.properties gives name=Willy. age is not in Penguin_en.properties so it falls back to Penguin.properties giving age=1.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 519–525

text: Which of the following correctly creates a Locale for French spoken in France?
explanation: All three create a Locale representing French in France. new Locale(String, String) is the legacy constructor. Locale.of(String, String) is the Java 19+ factory method. Locale.FRANCE is a predefined constant for fr_FR.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 505–509

text: What does the following code print in the US locale?
explanation: NumberFormat.getCurrencyInstance(Locale.US) formats numbers as US currency, using $ symbol, comma grouping separators, and period as decimal separator.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 511–515

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: MessageFormat.format takes only one argument.

explanation: MessageFormat.format(pattern, args...) substitutes {0}, {1}, etc. with the corresponding arguments. {0} is replaced by "Alice" and {1} by 5.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 525–528

text: Which DateTimeFormatter pattern correctly formats a date as 'Jan 31, 2024'?
explanation: In DateTimeFormatter patterns: M or MM formats month as number, MMM formats month as abbreviated name (Jan), MMMM formats month as full name (January). 'Jan 31, 2024' uses MMM (abbreviated month).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 515–519

text: Which of the following are true about ResourceBundle? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: ResourceBundle.getBundle() uses a lookup hierarchy based on locale.
ResourceBundle falls back to the default locale if the specific locale bundle is not found.
A missing key in the bundle always returns null.
The base bundle (no locale suffix) is the last resort in lookup.
ResourceBundle can be backed by .properties files or Java classes.

explanation: ResourceBundle uses a hierarchical lookup: specific locale → default locale → base bundle. If a key is not found in any bundle, MissingResourceException is thrown (not null). Bundles can be .properties files or ListResourceBundle subclasses.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 519–525

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: In German locale (de_DE), the period is the grouping separator and the comma is the decimal separator. 1234567.89 formats as 1.234.567,89.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 511–515

text: Which of the following predefined DateTimeFormatter instances is locale-sensitive?
explanation: ofLocalizedDate(FormatStyle) produces a formatter that is locale-sensitive — the output varies by locale. ISO_LOCAL_DATE and ISO_DATE_TIME always use ISO-8601 format regardless of locale. ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd") produces a fixed pattern.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 515–519

text: What is the result of the following code?
explanation: NumberFormat.parse() converts a locale-formatted string to a Number. In US locale, comma is the grouping separator and period is the decimal. '1,234.56' parses to 1234.56 (a Double).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 511–515

text: Which of the following are valid ways to specify a Locale in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Locale.of() is the Java 19+ factory (replaces deprecated new Locale()). Locale.Builder provides a fluent API. Locale.forLanguageTag() parses IETF BCP 47 tags. Locale.create() does not exist.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 505–509

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The pattern 'dd/MM/yyyy' formats day/month/year with zero-padding. March 15, 2024 formats as 15/03/2024.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 515–519

text: Which of the following correctly parses a date string using DateTimeFormatter?
explanation: LocalDate.parse(String, DateTimeFormatter) and DateTimeFormatter.parse(String) both work. The first gives a LocalDate directly; the second returns a TemporalAccessor that you'd query. In practice, A is the most common. LocalDate.of() does not accept a String.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 515–519

text: What exception is thrown when a key is missing from a ResourceBundle?
explanation: ResourceBundle.getString() throws MissingResourceException (a RuntimeException) when the specified key is not found in any bundle in the lookup chain. It never returns null.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 519–521

text: Which of the following statements about locale-sensitive operations are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: String comparison with equals() is locale-sensitive.
DateTimeFormatter with ISO patterns is locale-sensitive.

explanation: String.toUpperCase(Locale) applies locale rules (e.g., Turkish 'i' → 'İ'). Collator compares strings per locale. NumberFormat produces locale-specific output. String.equals() compares Unicode code points and is NOT locale-sensitive. ISO DateTimeFormatter patterns produce the same output regardless of locale.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 11 – Exceptions and Localization, pp. 508–511

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Period.toString() uses ISO-8601 duration format. P1Y2M3D means Period of 1 Year, 2 Months, 3 Days. This is NOT locale-sensitive — it always uses the ISO format.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 186–190
